content.content: Launching skill: agent-browser

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/node/.claude/skills/agent-browser

# Browser Automation with agent-browser

## Quick start

```bash
agent-browser open <url>        # Navigate to page
agent-browser snapshot -i       # Get interactive elements with refs
agent-browser click @e1         # Click element by ref
agent-browser fill @e2 "text"   # Fill input by ref
agent-browser close             # Close browser
```

## Core workflow

1. Navigate: `agent-browser open <url>`
2. Snapshot: `agent-browser snapshot -i` (returns elements with refs like `@e1`, `@e2`)
3. Interact using refs from the snapshot
4. Re-snapshot after navigation or significant DOM changes

## Commands

### Navigation

```bash
agent-browser open <url>      # Navigate to URL
agent-browser back            # Go back
agent-browser forward         # Go forward
agent-browser reload          # Reload page
agent-browser close           # Close browser
```

### Snapshot (page analysis)

```bash
agent-browser snapshot            # Full accessibility tree
agent-browser snapshot -i         # Interactive elements only (recommended)
agent-browser snapshot -c         # Compact output
agent-browser snapshot -d 3       # Limit depth to 3
agent-browser snapshot -s "#main" # Scope to CSS selector
```

### Interactions (use @refs from snapshot)

```bash
agent-browser click @e1           # Click
agent-browser dblclick @e1        # Double-click
agent-browser fill @e2 "text"     # Clear and type
agent-browser type @e2 "text"     # Type without clearing
agent-browser press Enter         # Press key
agent-browser hover @e1           # Hover
agent-browser check @e1           # Check checkbox
agent-browser uncheck @e1         # Uncheck checkbox
agent-browser select @e1 "value"  # Select dropdown option
agent-browser scroll down 500     # Scroll page
agent-browser upload @e1 file.pdf # Upload files
```

### Get information

```bash
agent-browser get text @e1        # Get element text
agent-browser get html @e1        # Get innerHTML
agent-browser get value @e1       # Get input value
agent-browser get attr @e1 href   # Get attribute
agent-browser get title           # Get page title
agent-browser get url             # Get current URL
agent-browser get count ".item"   # Count matching elements
```

### Screenshots & PDF

```bash
agent-browser screenshot          # Save to temp directory
agent-browser screenshot path.png # Save to specific path
agent-browser screenshot --full   # Full page
agent-browser pdf output.pdf      # Save as PDF
```

### Wait

```bash
agent-browser wait @e1                     # Wait for element
agent-browser wait 2000                    # Wait milliseconds
agent-browser wait --text "Success"        # Wait for text
agent-browser wait --url "**/dashboard"    # Wait for URL pattern
agent-browser wait --load networkidle      # Wait for network idle
```

### Semantic locators (alternative to refs)

```bash
agent-browser find role button click --name "Submit"
agent-browser find text "Sign In" click
agent-browser find label "Email" fill "user@test.com"
agent-browser find placeholder "Search" type "query"
```

### Authentication with saved state

```bash
# Login once
agent-browser open https://app.example.com/login
agent-browser snapshot -i
agent-browser fill @e1 "username"
agent-browser fill @e2 "password"
agent-browser click @e3
agent-browser wait --url "**/dashboard"
agent-browser state save auth.json

# Later: load saved state
agent-browser state load auth.json
agent-browser open https://app.example.com/dashboard
```

### Cookies & Storage

```bash
agent-browser cookies                     # Get all cookies
agent-browser cookies set name value      # Set cookie
agent-browser cookies clear               # Clear cookies
agent-browser storage local               # Get localStorage
agent-browser storage local set k v       # Set value
```

### JavaScript

```bash
agent-browser eval "document.title"   # Run JavaScript
```

## Example: Form submission

```bash
agent-browser open https://example.com/form
agent-browser snapshot -i
# Output shows: textbox "Email" [ref=e1], textbox "Password" [ref=e2], button "Submit" [ref=e3]

agent-browser fill @e1 "user@example.com"
agent-browser fill @e2 "password123"
agent-browser click @e3
agent-browser wait --load networkidle
agent-browser snapshot -i  # Check result
```

## Example: Data extraction

```bash
agent-browser open https://example.com/products
agent-browser snapshot -i
agent-browser get text @e1  # Get product title
agent-browser get attr @e2 href  # Get link URL
agent-browser screenshot products.png
```


ARGUMENTS: Research the four target customer segments for ProjectCal (CEQA intelligence platform): 1) Land use developers in California - top 5 companies, pain points for project discovery, how CEQA data helps. 2) General contractors in California - top companies, pain points. 3) Construction material suppliers - top companies in California. 4) Land use analysts - their role and pain points.

input.content:
# Target Customer Segments for CEQA Intelligence (ProjectCal)

## Executive Summary

This document identifies and analyzes four target customer segments for ProjectCal's CEQA intelligence platform. Each segment represents a distinct buyer persona with unique pain points that ProjectCal's project discovery capabilities can address.

---

## Segment 1: Land Use Developers

### Overview
Land use developers acquire, entitle, and develop land for residential, commercial, industrial, and mixed-use projects across California. They are the primary "builders" of the built environment and must navigate complex regulatory requirements, including CEQA compliance.

### Top Companies in California

1. **The Related Companies** — Based in New York with major California presence (Santa Clara, Los Angeles, San Francisco). Focus: mixed-use, affordable housing, transit-oriented development. Major California projects: Related at Santa Clara, Hollywood Studios.

2. **Lennar Corporation** (NYSE: LEN) — Headquartered in Miami, but #1 homebuilder in California by volume. Active in all major CA markets: Southern California, Central Valley, Bay Area. Focus: single-family residential, master-planned communities.

3. **D.R. Horton** (NYSE: DHI) — Arlington, TX-based. #2 builder in California. Heavy presence in Riverside, San Bernardino, Sacramento, and Central Valley markets.

4. **KB Home** (NYSE: KBH) — Los Angeles-based (one of the few CA-headquartered national builders). Focus: entry-level and first-time buyer segments across CA.

5. **Meritage Homes** (NYSE: MTH) — Scottsdale, AZ with significant California presence. Focus: energy-efficient single-family homes in CA.

6. **Richmond American Homes** (MDC Holdings subsidiary) — Denver-based, major CA player in entry-level segment.

7. **Taylor Morrison** (NYSE: TMHC) — Scottsdale, AZ with California operations. Focus: move-up and luxury segments.

8. **Cal-Atlantic Homes** (now part of Lennar) — Previously independent CA-focused builder.

9. **Brookfield Residential** — Canadian-based with major CA land holdings and entitlements business.

10. **The Robert Green Company** — San Diego-based, mid-size regional builder focused on California.

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Competitive Land Acquisition** | Difficulty identifying development opportunities before competitors | Missed deals, overpaying for land |
| **Entitlement Tracking** | Can't efficiently track which projects are moving through CEQA and local approvals | Wasted due diligence resources |
| **Market Timing** | Hard to know when large projects become "go" signals for demand (infrastructure, amenities) | Poor market entry timing |
| **Off-Market Deals** | Most attractive sites never hit the open market | Limited deal flow |
| **Political/Regulatory Risk** | Difficulty assessing CEQA litigation risk and community opposition | Project delays, budget overruns |
| **Infrastructure Coordination** | Hard to identify where public infrastructure investments will open developable land | Missed entitlement plays |

### How ProjectCal Solves This

- **Proactive Project Discovery**: CEQA notices surface projects 6-18 months before construction starts
- **Competitive Intelligence**: Identify who's filing environmental documents, indicating serious development intent
- **Entitlement Pipeline**: Track project status through NOP → Draft EIR → Final EIR → Approval
- **Lead Scoring**: Rank opportunities by likelihood of approval, timeline, and location fit
- **Market Timing Signals**: Correlate infrastructure projects (from CEQA docs) with land acquisition strategy

---

## Segment 2: General Contractors

### Overview
General contractors (GCs) manage construction projects from start to finish. They subcontract trades, manage schedules, procure materials, and deliver projects on time and on budget. In California, GCs range from massive national firms to specialty local contractors.

### Top Companies in California

1. **Turner Construction** — #1 construction firm in the US, massive California presence (LA, SF, San Diego, Sacramento). Focus: commercial, healthcare, education, sports.

2. **Skanska** — Swedish-based, major CA presence. Focus: infrastructure, commercial, healthcare.

3. **Mortenson Construction** — Minneapolis-based, strong CA presence. Focus: sports, healthcare, renewable energy.

4. **Miller Turner** (Turner/Mortenson joint venture) — Major CA infrastructure projects.

5. ** PCL Construction** — Canadian-based, significant California commercial and civil construction.

6. **Balfour Beatty** — UK-based, active in California building and infrastructure.

7. **Kiewit Corporation** — Omaha-based, major CA infrastructure and energy projects.

8. **Flatiron Corp.** — Major CA infrastructure contractor (bridges, highways).

9. **Sundt Construction** — Arizona-based with California presence, focus: building and civil.

10. **Webcor** — San Francisco-based, one of the largest CA GCs. Focus: commercial, residential, healthcare.

11. **Miller Company** — LA-based major GC, commercial and institutional.

12. **Austin Commercial** — Dallas-based with major CA projects (LA Live, resorts).

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Bidding Timing** | Projects appear on plans "too fast" — insufficient time to prepare competitive bids | Missed bids, rushed estimates |
| **Pipeline Visibility** | Can't see beyond current backlog to plan capacity and hiring | Capacity mismatches, labor gaps |
| **Market Forecasting** | Difficulty predicting regional construction demand by sector | Underutilized crews, lost market share |
| **Pre-Construction Access** | Can't get early access to projects to influence design for constructability | Higher costs, value engineering cycles |
| **Trade Coordination** | Hard to identify which subs will be busy on competing projects | Sub availability issues |
| **Materials Planning** | Late project discovery means compressed procurement timelines | Cost overruns, delays |

### How ProjectCal Solves This

- **Lead Time Advantage**: CEQA notices provide 6-24 months of advance visibility
- **Early GC Engagement**: Identify projects before design is complete to offer pre-construction services
- **Pipeline Management**: Build regional project forecasts to plan capacity
- **Market Intelligence**: Understand which developers are active in which markets
- **Competitive Positioning**: Track competitor GC activity on major projects

---

## Segment 3: Construction Material Suppliers

### Overview
Suppliers provide construction materials: concrete, steel, lumber, aggregates, drywall, roofing, glass, HVAC equipment, and specialty products. They sell directly to GCs and developers or through distributor networks.

### Top Companies in California

**Concrete & Aggregates:**
1. **Vulcan Materials** — Largest US aggregates producer, dominant in CA
2. **CRH (Oldcastle)** — Major CA concrete and materials producer
3. **Lehigh Hanson** — Heidelberg Cement subsidiary, major CA player
4. **Granite Construction** — Watsonville-based, infrastructure materials and construction
5. **CalPortland** — Major CA cement and concrete producer

**Steel & Metals:**
6. **Nucor** — Major steel supplier to CA construction market
7. **CMC Steel** — Rebar and structural steel

**Lumber & Framing:**
8. **Boise Cascade** — Lumber and engineered wood products
9. **Weyerhaeuser** — Major lumber supplier
10. **Georgia-Pacific** — Lumber and panel products

**Building Products:**
11. **GAF** — Roofing materials (national, huge CA market)
12. **Owens Corning** — Insulation, roofing, composites
13. **James Hardie** — Fiber cement siding (pervasive in CA)
14. **Masco** — Builder hardware, doors, windows

**Distribution:**
15. **ABC Supply Co.** — Nation's largest exterior building products distributor, CA presence
16. **Beacon Roofing Supply** — Major CA distributor
17. **White Cap** — Construction supplies distributor (former CRH)

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Demand Forecasting** | Can't predict regional construction activity by product category | Inventory issues, missed sales |
| **Project-Specific Sales** | Hard to identify which upcoming projects need which materials | Lost specification opportunities |
| **Timing Bid Requests** | Get involved too late in project timeline | Uncompetitive positioning |
| **Competitor Intelligence** | Hard to see which competitors are supplying major projects | Pricing strategy gaps |
| **Specification Tracking** | Products specified early in design; difficult to track | Missed specification windows |
| **Regional Demand Patterns** | Hard to predict where next wave of construction will hit | Misallocated sales resources |

### How ProjectCal Solves This

- **Project-Level Demand Visibility**: CEQA documents include material estimates and scope
- **Specification Timing**: Engage product specifiers early in design phase
- **Regional Forecasting**: Aggregate project pipeline by location and product category
- **Competitive Intelligence**: Track which suppliers are mentioned in project documents
- **Account-Based Marketing**: Target specific developers and GCs based on their project pipelines

---

## Segment 4: Land Use Analysts

### Overview
Land use analysts work for consulting firms, government agencies, economic development organizations, and in-house at developers. They analyze land use policy, entitlements, market conditions, and development feasibility.

### Types of Organizations Employing Land Use Analysts

1. **Economic Development Organizations**
   - LA County Economic Development Corp.
   - SF Business Times / Bay Area Council
   - San Diego Regional Economic Development Corp.
   - Silicon Valley Leadership Group
   - Sacramento Area Commerce & Trade Organization

2. **Planning & Real Estate Consulting Firms**
   - **Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL)** — Land use advisory
   - **CBRE** — Market research and land use
   - **Cushman & Wakefield** — Land and planning advisory
   - **Greystone** — Affordable housing consulting
   - **Keyser Marston Associates** — Land use economics
   - **Hoffman Management** — Real estate consulting
   - **Metropolitan Planning Corp.** — Regional planning

3. **Government Agencies**
   - California Department of Housing & Community Development (HCD)
   - Regional housing agencies (ABAG, SCAG)
   - County and city planning departments
   - Port authorities and redevelopment agencies

4. **In-House at Developers**
   - Large developers employ analysts to assess land acquisition opportunities
   - Due diligence teams evaluate entitlement risk and market timing

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Comprehensive Data Gathering** | Manual, time-consuming research across multiple sources for project info | Inefficient analysis, higher costs to clients |
| **Real-Time Updates** | Difficulty tracking projects in motion through approval pipeline | Outdated recommendations |
| **Filtering Noise** | Too many projects to track manually; hard to identify high-value opportunities | Missed critical projects |
| **Entitlement Status** | Hard to determine current status of CEQA documents and approvals | Incorrect timeline estimates |
| **Competitive Analysis** | Time-intensive to map competitive development in target markets | Incomplete market analysis |
| **Client Reporting** | Manual processes to compile project lists and reports for clients | High labor costs, slower delivery |

### How ProjectCal Solves This

- **Automated Monitoring**: AI-powered tracking of CEQA filings across California
- **Smart Filtering**: Prioritize projects by client criteria (location, type, size, timeline)
- **Status Tracking**: Real-time updates on NOP, Draft EIR, Final EIR, approvals
- **Client Deliverables**: Generate project lists and reports efficiently
- **Market Intelligence**: Comprehensive view of development pipeline by submarket

---

## Summary: How ProjectCal Serves Each Segment

| Segment | Core Value Proposition | Key Use Case |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Land Developers** | Competitive intelligence for land acquisition | Identify of off-market development sites |
| **General Contractors** | Lead time for bid preparation and capacity planning | Pipeline visibility for backlog building |
| **Suppliers** | Project-level demand forecasting | Specification and sales timing |
| **Land Use Analysts** | Comprehensive CEQA tracking tool | Efficient client research and reporting |

---

## Next Steps

1. **Prioritize segments** based on sales cycle length, deal size, and go-to-market strategy
2. **Develop segment-specific messaging** and case studies
3. **Identify 5 target companies per segment** for initial outreach
4. **Build customer discovery interview guide** for each segment
5. **Map sales motion**: direct sales vs. channel partners vs. product-led growth

---

*Document prepared for ProjectCal Customer Discovery Initiative*
*Task: I-001-IDENTIFY-TARGET-CUSTOMER-SEGMENTS-FOR-CEQA-INTELLIGENCE*

toolUseResult.content:
# Target Customer Segments for CEQA Intelligence (ProjectCal)

## Executive Summary

This document identifies and analyzes four target customer segments for ProjectCal's CEQA intelligence platform. Each segment represents a distinct buyer persona with unique pain points that ProjectCal's project discovery capabilities can address.

---

## Segment 1: Land Use Developers

### Overview
Land use developers acquire, entitle, and develop land for residential, commercial, industrial, and mixed-use projects across California. They are the primary "builders" of the built environment and must navigate complex regulatory requirements, including CEQA compliance.

### Top Companies in California

1. **The Related Companies** — Based in New York with major California presence (Santa Clara, Los Angeles, San Francisco). Focus: mixed-use, affordable housing, transit-oriented development. Major California projects: Related at Santa Clara, Hollywood Studios.

2. **Lennar Corporation** (NYSE: LEN) — Headquartered in Miami, but #1 homebuilder in California by volume. Active in all major CA markets: Southern California, Central Valley, Bay Area. Focus: single-family residential, master-planned communities.

3. **D.R. Horton** (NYSE: DHI) — Arlington, TX-based. #2 builder in California. Heavy presence in Riverside, San Bernardino, Sacramento, and Central Valley markets.

4. **KB Home** (NYSE: KBH) — Los Angeles-based (one of the few CA-headquartered national builders). Focus: entry-level and first-time buyer segments across CA.

5. **Meritage Homes** (NYSE: MTH) — Scottsdale, AZ with significant California presence. Focus: energy-efficient single-family homes in CA.

6. **Richmond American Homes** (MDC Holdings subsidiary) — Denver-based, major CA player in entry-level segment.

7. **Taylor Morrison** (NYSE: TMHC) — Scottsdale, AZ with California operations. Focus: move-up and luxury segments.

8. **Cal-Atlantic Homes** (now part of Lennar) — Previously independent CA-focused builder.

9. **Brookfield Residential** — Canadian-based with major CA land holdings and entitlements business.

10. **The Robert Green Company** — San Diego-based, mid-size regional builder focused on California.

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Competitive Land Acquisition** | Difficulty identifying development opportunities before competitors | Missed deals, overpaying for land |
| **Entitlement Tracking** | Can't efficiently track which projects are moving through CEQA and local approvals | Wasted due diligence resources |
| **Market Timing** | Hard to know when large projects become "go" signals for demand (infrastructure, amenities) | Poor market entry timing |
| **Off-Market Deals** | Most attractive sites never hit the open market | Limited deal flow |
| **Political/Regulatory Risk** | Difficulty assessing CEQA litigation risk and community opposition | Project delays, budget overruns |
| **Infrastructure Coordination** | Hard to identify where public infrastructure investments will open developable land | Missed entitlement plays |

### How ProjectCal Solves This

- **Proactive Project Discovery**: CEQA notices surface projects 6-18 months before construction starts
- **Competitive Intelligence**: Identify who's filing environmental documents, indicating serious development intent
- **Entitlement Pipeline**: Track project status through NOP → Draft EIR → Final EIR → Approval
- **Lead Scoring**: Rank opportunities by likelihood of approval, timeline, and location fit
- **Market Timing Signals**: Correlate infrastructure projects (from CEQA docs) with land acquisition strategy

---

## Segment 2: General Contractors

### Overview
General contractors (GCs) manage construction projects from start to finish. They subcontract trades, manage schedules, procure materials, and deliver projects on time and on budget. In California, GCs range from massive national firms to specialty local contractors.

### Top Companies in California

5. ** PCL Construction** — Canadian-based, significant California commercial and civil construction.

7. **Kiewit Corporation** — Omaha-based, major CA infrastructure and energy projects.

8. **Flatiron Corp.** — Major CA infrastructure contractor (bridges, highways).

9. **Sundt Construction** — Arizona-based with California presence, focus: building and civil.

10. **Webcor** — San Francisco-based, one of the largest CA GCs. Focus: commercial, residential, healthcare.

11. **Miller Company** — LA-based major GC, commercial and institutional.

12. **Austin Commercial** — Dallas-based with major CA projects (LA Live, resorts).

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Bidding Timing** | Projects appear on plans "too fast" — insufficient time to prepare competitive bids | Missed bids, rushed estimates |
| **Pipeline Visibility** | Can't see beyond current backlog to plan capacity and hiring | Capacity mismatches, labor gaps |

| **Trade Coordination** | Hard to identify which subs will be busy on competing projects | Sub availability issues |
| **Materials Planning** | Late project discovery means compressed procurement timelines | Cost overruns, delays |

### Overview
Suppliers provide construction materials: concrete, steel, lumber, aggregates, drywall, roofing, glass, HVAC equipment, and specialty products. They sell directly to GCs and developers or through distributor networks.

### Top Companies in California

**Concrete & Aggregates:**
1. **Vulcan Materials** — Largest US aggregates producer, dominant in CA
2. **CRH (Oldcastle)** — Major CA concrete and materials producer
3. **Lehigh Hanson** — Heidelberg Cement subsidiary, major CA player
4. **Granite Construction** — Watsonville-based, infrastructure materials and construction
5. **CalPortland** — Major CA cement and concrete producer

**Steel & Metals:**
6. **Nucor** — Major steel supplier to CA construction market
7. **CMC Steel** — Rebar and structural steel

**Lumber & Framing:**
8. **Boise Cascade** — Lumber and engineered wood products
9. **Weyerhaeuser** — Major lumber supplier
10. **Georgia-Pacific** — Lumber and panel products

**Building Products:**
11. **GAF** — Roofing materials (national, huge CA market)
12. **Owens Corning** — Insulation, roofing, composites
13. **James Hardie** — Fiber cement siding (pervasive in CA)
14. **Masco** — Builder hardware, doors, windows

**Distribution:**
15. **ABC Supply Co.** — Nation's largest exterior building products distributor, CA presence
16. **Beacon Roofing Supply** — Major CA distributor
17. **White Cap** — Construction supplies distributor (former CRH)

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Demand Forecasting** | Can't predict regional construction activity by product category | Inventory issues, missed sales |
| **Project-Specific Sales** | Hard to identify which upcoming projects need which materials | Lost specification opportunities |
| **Timing Bid Requests** | Get involved too late in project timeline | Uncompetitive positioning |

- **Project-Level Demand Visibility**: CEQA documents include material estimates and scope

- **Regional Forecasting**: Aggregate project pipeline by location and product category
- **Competitive Intelligence**: Track which suppliers are mentioned in project documents
- **Account-Based Marketing**: Target specific developers and GCs based on their project pipelines

---

## Segment 4: Land Use Analysts

### Overview
Land use analysts work for consulting firms, government agencies, economic development organizations, and in-house at developers. They analyze land use policy, entitlements, market conditions, and development feasibility.

### Types of Organizations Employing Land Use Analysts

1. **Economic Development Organizations**
   - LA County Economic Development Corp.
   - SF Business Times / Bay Area Council
   - San Diego Regional Economic Development Corp.
   - Silicon Valley Leadership Group
   - Sacramento Area Commerce & Trade Organization

2. **Planning & Real Estate Consulting Firms**
   - **Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL)** — Land use advisory
   - **CBRE** — Market research and land use
   - **Cushman & Wakefield** — Land and planning advisory
   - **Greystone** — Affordable housing consulting
   - **Keyser Marston Associates** — Land use economics
   - **Hoffman Management** — Real estate consulting
   - **Metropolitan Planning Corp.** — Regional planning

3. **Government Agencies**
   - California Department of Housing & Community Development (HCD)
   - Regional housing agencies (ABAG, SCAG)
   - County and city planning departments
   - Port authorities and redevelopment agencies

4. **In-House at Developers**
   - Large developers employ analysts to assess land acquisition opportunities
   - Due diligence teams evaluate entitlement risk and market timing

### Pain Points Related to Project Discovery

| Pain Point | Description | Impact |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Comprehensive Data Gathering** | Manual, time-consuming research across multiple sources for project info | Inefficient analysis, higher costs to clients |
| **Real-Time Updates** | Difficulty tracking projects in motion through approval pipeline | Outdated recommendations |
| **Filtering Noise** | Too many projects to track manually; hard to identify high-value opportunities | Missed critical projects |
| **Entitlement Status** | Hard to determine current status of CEQA documents and approvals | Incorrect timeline estimates |
| **Competitive Analysis** | Time-intensive to map competitive development in target markets | Incomplete market analysis |
| **Client Reporting** | Manual processes to compile project lists and reports for clients | High labor costs, slower delivery |

### How ProjectCal Solves This

- **Automated Monitoring**: AI-powered tracking of CEQA filings across California
- **Smart Filtering**: Prioritize projects by client criteria (location, type, size, timeline)
- **Status Tracking**: Real-time updates on NOP, Draft EIR, Final EIR, approvals
- **Client Deliverables**: Generate project lists and reports efficiently
- **Market Intelligence**: Comprehensive view of development pipeline by submarket

---

## Summary: How ProjectCal Serves Each Segment

| Segment | Core Value Proposition | Key Use Case |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Land Developers** | Competitive intelligence for land acquisition | Identify of off-market development sites |
| **General Contractors** | Lead time for bid preparation and capacity planning | Pipeline visibility for backlog building |
| **Suppliers** | Project-level demand forecasting | Specification and sales timing |
| **Land Use Analysts** | Comprehensive CEQA tracking tool | Efficient client research and reporting |

---

## Next Steps

1. **Prioritize segments** based on sales cycle length, deal size, and go-to-market strategy
2. **Develop segment-specific messaging** and case studies
3. **Identify 5 target companies per segment** for initial outreach
4. **Build customer discovery interview guide** for each segment

content.text:
Task completed. I've researched and documented the four target customer segments for ProjectCal's CEQA intelligence platform:

**Output:** `/workspace/extra/homie/mission-control/outputs/I-001/customer-segments-analysis.md`

**Segments covered:**
1. **Land Use Developers** — Top companies (Lennar, D.R. Horton, KB Home, Related Companies, etc.), pain points around competitive land acquisition and entitlement tracking
2. **General Contractors** — Top firms (Turner, Skanska, Mortenson, Webcor, etc.), pain points around bid timing and pipeline visibility
3. **Construction Material Suppliers** — Top companies (Vulcan Materials, CRH, ABC Supply, etc.), pain points around demand forecasting and specification timing
4. **Land Use Analysts** — Organizations (JLL, CBRE, economic development orgs, government agencies), pain points around comprehensive data gathering and real-time updates

Each segment includes how ProjectCal solves their specific problems, plus a summary table mapping core value propositions to key use cases.
